reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware interpretation of pressure-contribution ratios at venting and what (non)linearity implies about reaction modeling, with correct choices narrowly supported by the stated quantitative comparisons and qualitative trend descriptions. Decoys are mostly single-fault and technically plausible, though two options (B, F) lean on the same nonlinearity misread, and wording like “dominant contributor” vs “meaningfully contributes” may allow mild stylistic cueing. Overall it should discriminate experts who avoid over-interpreting linear segments and who keep contribution hierarchy distinct from absolute negligibility.

The item cleanly targets whether a chemistry-agnostic SOC threshold is admissible given explicitly different SOC–flammability functional forms (LCO step-change vs LFP gradual rise) and composition-driven hazard limits, so the epistemic boundary is tight and the mechanism-based limitation is naturally elicited. It has strong discrimination because it punishes the common overreach of treating volume fraction or an averaged SOC point as universal, while still being answerable from the prompt’s stated contrasts. Minor risk remains that it implicitly asks for extra-chemistry generalization (mentioning other chemistries/composition effects) without fully specifying how far the solver must go, but this does not dominate the core judgment axis.
